data.title: DeFi technology is a complement to centralized finance, Fed's Waller says
data.description:
Federal Reserve Governor Christopher Waller argued that decentralized finance (DeFi) technology can work alongside traditional financial systems, rather than replace them, he said on Friday.

"The bottom line is that things like [distributed ledger technology], tokenization, and smart contracts are just technologies for trading that can be used in defi or also to improve efficiency in centralized finance," Waller said in a prepared speech at the Institute of Advanced Studies’ Vienna Macroeconomics Workshop.

Distributed ledger technology, for instance, provides a more efficient and faster way to track the transfer of assets. "We already see several financial institutions experimenting with DLT for traditional repo trading that occurs 24/7," the Fed governor pointed out.

"While there are certain services emerging through DeFi that cannot be provided by centralized finance, the technological innovations stemming from DeFi are largely complementary to centralized finance," he said.

Stablecoins can, too, compliment centralized finance by reducing the need for payment intermediaries, and, therefore, lowering costs of payments worldwide. The safety of this kind of cryptocurrency, Waller noted, "is not assured."

"If appropriate guardrails can be erected to minimize run risk and mitigate other risks, such as their potential use in illicit finance, then stablecoins may have benefits in payments and by serving as a safe asset on a variety of new trading platforms,” he added.

data.title: Netflix buoys Nasdaq, Dow wavers on AmEx; S&P heads for best weekly win streak of 2024
data.description:
Wall Street on Friday was largely higher, as market participants digested a slew of quarterly reports. A post-earnings surge in streaming giant Netflix (NFLX) and a climb in Apple (AAPL) on some positive China news was countered by a slide in healthcare service firm CVS (CVS) and Dow 30 component American Express (AXP).

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was +0.31% at 5,859.43 points in midday trade, less than 20 points shy of its intraday record high. The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +0.54% at 18,472.83 points. Both gauges were boosted by Netflix (NFLX). Conversely, AmEx's (AXP) dip put pressure on the Dow (DJI), with the blue-chip index -0.03% at 43,226.64 points.

All 11 S&P sectors were in the green except Energy.

Netflix (NFLX) was +10%, as analysts and investors cheered the streaming leader's quarterly report that easily topped expectations for profit and subscriber growth. Netflix (NFLX) added 5.07M global paid net members in Q3 to take its total membership to 282.72M.

Apple (AAPL) was +1.2%, after Counterpoint Research data showed that the tech behemoth's iPhone 16 sales rose 20% in China since their debut. Wedbush Securities suggested that the development showed a "renaissance of growth" had begun for Apple (AAPL).

Meanwhile, CVS Health (CVS) was -8.6% and was the top percentage loser on the S&P 500 (SP500). The Woonsocket, R.I.-based company operates pharmacies, CVS Caremark - one of the largest U.S. pharmacy benefits managers - and also offers health insurance.

Amid activist pressure, CVS (CVS) announced the replacement of its top boss Karen Lynch with company veteran and Caremark president David Joyner. It also withdrew its previously provided 2024 guidance.

American Express (AXP) was -3.0% and was another top S&P percentage loser, after the credit card issuer slightly trimmed its full-year revenue growth guidance.

Quarterly results were the main focus on Friday, and will continue to be so next week in which a deluge of reports will be published. Solid bank earnings this week, along with economic data that reinforced soft landing hopes has put the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) on track for a six-week win streak, which would also mark the index's longest weekly positive run in 2024.

"Following the recent strength in the jobs report as well as upward revisions to GDP, the market has rightfully walked back expectations of a more aggressive easing cycle. If neutral is 3.5-4.0% (100 bps above inflation), that’s at most another 4-5 cuts from here," Fidelity's Jurrien Timmer said on X (formerly Twitter) on Thursday.

Timmer also highlighted "the Fed’s tentative success in landing this plane without crashing it. The pandemic-era excess demand for labor has been completely worked off without elevating jobless claims or causing a downturn in the Fed’s weekly economic index. If the Fed can stop the pendulum here, this cycle could go down as a rare success."

Friday's economic calendar was fairly light. Data from the U.S. Census Bureau showed housing starts and building permits unexpectedly slumped in September.

"Housing starts & permits were softer, but the main story is that housing starts continue to run below the level of completions, which means units under construction will continue to decline, weighing on residential investment," Renaissance Macro Research said on X.

Turning to the fixed-income markets, U.S. Treasury yields were lower. The benchmark 10-year yield (US10Y) was down 3 basis points to 4.07%, while the shorter-end 2-year yield (US2Y) was down 1 basis point to 3.97%.

For more, see how Treasury yields have done across the curve on the Seeking Alpha bond page.

More on the markets

  • Is The Market's Melt-Up Due To FOMO?
  • Subsets And Sensibility
  • Gold And The Dow Both Trade At All-Time Highs: What History Says
  • UBS upgrades view on U.S. stocks to attractive - here are its top picks
  • Oppenheimer buy-rated ideas: BSX, MDT, and one hold: CYTK

data.title: Stifel’s chief economist: The Fed sent the wrong message with 50 bp rate cut
data.description:
The Federal Reserve sent “the wrong message with a 50 basis point cut,” said Lindsay Piegza, chief economist at Stifel.

During a Bloomberg interview, Piegza said that the 50 basis points interest rate cut was outsized, and that now the Fed is trying to recalibrate market expectations and to determine what the appropriate next step will be.

“It was an inappropriate message to the market to suggest that they were in a rush, to not only get back to neutral, but potentially below to provide support to an ailing economy,” she said, and added that a softer 25 basis point cut, would have meant that the Fed is simply removing fiscal policy “at a slow and tempered pace back towards neutral as the data neutralizes.”

Piegza argued that the market is seeing some underlying metrics re-accelerate, pushing away from the Fed's 2% target, such as the core CPE, which is currently at 2.68 as of August from 2.65 in July.

“The Fed [is] motivated by this fear of impending weakness in the labor market, [and has] shifted their focus away from inflation,” she said. “Now, we see that the labor market strength appears to be well founded… The focus of the Fed should go back to getting inflation under control.”

“A soft landing is not just about maintaining positive growth but reinstating price stability at 2%, and we still have quite a way to go before we can achieve that goal.”

She said that the Fed moved its focus away from inflation prematurely, leaving the markets in an uncomfortable position as growth remains solid.

“If we do start to lose momentum – looking out to 2025, 2026 – and the Fed continues to tolerate above-target inflation, the biggest risk for the U.S. economy longer-term is not a downturn, like we're seeing in our developed counterparts abroad, but a period of stagflation.”

International commodity markets, higher prices for energy, and the upcoming U.S. election “regardless of which side of the aisle we find ourselves on,” are upside risks to inflation, she said.

“Either candidate is proposing expansionary fiscal policy, which will continue to complicate the Fed's ability to get back to that 2% level of inflation.”

Bond yields:

  • U.S. 5-Year Bond Yield (US5Y) – 3.89%
  • U. S. 10-Year Bond Yield (US10Y) – 4.08%
  • U. S. 30-Year Bond Yield (US30Y) – 4.37%

data.title: Data center demand outpaces infrastructure: Oppenheimer
data.description:
The ever-increasing demand for data centers continues to outpace infrastructure and will likely lead to capacity constraints in 2025, according to Oppenheimer.

The rise of large language models and other artificial intelligence applications require extreme amounts of computing power, fueling the rampant rise in data center demand.

"The hyperscalers are still locked in an LLM war where there is a shortage of infrastructure for training, which is helping with datacenter pricing," said Oppenheimer analyst Timothy Horan, in a Friday investor note.

"Checks indicate extremely strong hyperscaler datacenter demand, and we will likely run out of capacity next year," he added. "Positively, enterprises realize data is the new oil, and will require/accelerate migration to the cloud to enable AI. Everyone is trying to balance latency, cost and accuracy, which often require smaller models."

In addition to the obvious winners of the data center race, which include Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT), Amazon Web Services (NASDAQ:AMZN) and Google (NASDAQ:GOOG)(NASDAQ:GOOGL), Oppenheimer also likes second derivative plays. These are infrastructure names such as Cogent Communications (NASDAQ:CCOI), DigitalOcean (NYSE:DOCN), Equinix (NASDAQ:EQIX) and Cloudflare (NYSE:NET).

"We also see companies like Amdocs (NASDAQ:DOX) benefiting as companies improve internal operations, like the telcos are doing," Horan noted. "NICE (NASDAQ:NICE) and Verint Systems (NASDAQ:VRNT) should also see use cases and adoption accelerate, but more so next year."

data.title: Bunge, ADM plunge as weak crop prices, farmer sentiment seen hurting demand
data.description:
Bunge (NYSE:BG) -6.4% and Archer Daniels Midland (NYSE:ADM) -3.5% in Friday's trading, possibly related to Raymond James' downgrade of grain handling peer Ag Growth International (OTCPK:AGGZF) to Market Perform from Outperform with a $62 price target, cut from $72, to reflect sustained macro headwinds across the agriculture complex such as weak crop prices and farmer sentiment; Ag Growth -10.6%.

Ray Jay analyst Steve Hansen says he is "increasingly concerned over short-to-medium term headwinds impacting its domestic portable business where dealer inventories are reportedly elevated."

After a brief September rally, crop prices have since turned lower, shedding most of their prior gains in response to healthy North American harvest expectations and global macro/trade uncertainty, and Hansen sees the lackluster backdrop continuing to weigh on grower purchase decisions, most notably for on-farm portable equipment.

Hansen says recent channel checks and conversations with management also suggest the sales malaise has caused dealer inventories of portable equipment to accumulate, likely introducing a short-to-medium term sales headwind that will drift into 2025.

data.title: Intel said to look for minority partners in programmable chip unit: report
data.description:
• Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) is looking to raise billions of dollars from minority partners for its programmable chip unit, known as Altera, CNBC reported.
  • Shares rose 0.7% in late morning trading on Friday, but have declined more than 50% year-to-date amid concerns about the viability of its turnaround prospects.
  • Santa Clara, Calif.-based Intel is looking for investors to provide capital to the unit, which makes chips for high-performance communications and data center applications, and would value Altera at $17B, the news outlet added, citing people familiar with the matter. The company has contacted private equity firms and strategic investors, the people added.
  • The Pat Gelsinger-led Intel has also told some of those investors that a majority stake in Altera could also be a possibility. Intel acquired Altera in 2015 for a sum of $16.7B.
  • Intel declined to comment when contacted by Seeking Alpha.
  • In October 2023, Intel said it would separate Altera into a standalone operation, effective January 1, 2024, in an effort to continue to simplify its larger business.

(This story has been updated with Intel's comment.)

data.title: Scotiabank continues to see positive tailwinds for U.S. Telcos ahead of Q3 report
data.description:
Scotiabank reiterated its positive view on major U.S. telecoms ahead of their third quarter report and continues to have a positive view on the industry, saying it remains supported by healthy subscriber loading dynamics and positive pricing trends.
The research firm noted that the industry is benefiting from lower interest rate expectations with continued positive money flow into the sector. "The setup in wireless is especially bullish with low industry churn, improving ARPU dynamics, low handset subsidization combined with declining capex spend," Scotiabank said in their October 18 note.
They said they remain constructive on wireless, but in cable, they expect loading to remain under pressure in the second half of this year.
For AT&T (NYSE:T), they expect Q3 revenue growth to be flat and adj. EBITDA growth of 1.9%, driven by mobility and consumer wireline, offset by decline in the business segment and mobility equipment revenues. They forecast the company to add 450K postpaid phone subs with ARPU growth of 1.0% and "industry-leading" wireless postpaid phone churn. They continue to have a positive outlook for AT&T, especially into 2025, anticipating improved consumer wireline growth and continued mid-single-digit EBITDA growth in mobility to begin to materially offset legacy business services. They kept their "Sector Outperform" investment rating on the company.
On Verizon (NYSE:VZ), they think it would be key for the company to continue to show y/y improvement in postpaid phone loading and lower leverage. For Q3, they expect VZ to report 220K postpaid phone net additions, with the consumer segment returning to growth after two quarters of share loss. They expect 2025 loading to show improved resilience, with all quarters next year showing positive loading. FCF conversion and growth will be another key metric into next year. "We believe investors are starting to put more focus on FCF generation vs. EBITDA as the industry undergoes a transformation with respect to capital spending plans and new financing structures to help fund future investments," they said.
Soctiabank believes T-Mobile's (NASDAQ:TMUS) medium-term outlook continues to be impressive with continuation of leading wireless subscriber loading, service revenue growth, and FCF growth compared to U.S. peers. They expect the company to report 700K postpaid phone net adds in Q3, EBITDA growth of 6.5%, and FCF generation of $4.7B. Albeit they continue to view the long-term growth prospects as very attractive, they lowered their rating to "sector perform" from "sector outperform."
For Comcast (NASDAQ:CMCSA), although consensus estimates should be achievable, pressure on broadband loading is likely to remain elevated due to ACP disconnections, but some offsets should be present in terms of seasonality as well, the research firm said. "All the topline growth in the quarter is expected to come from the content and experiences segment, as subscriber losses in connectivity will counter the expected ARPU growth. On the profitability side, we should see some margin pressure given the higher costs associated with the Olympics in addition to tougher comps on new attractions for theme parks and Studios... We continue to believe that broadband is the main driver for overall stock performance and Comcast," Scotiabank said.

data.title: Boston Scientific cut to Hold at Needham on growth concerns
data.description:
Needham downgraded Boston Scientific (NYSE:BSX) to Hold from Buy on Friday, noting a potential stagnation in its topline growth next year due to a slowdown in the company's electrophysiology business.

Analyst Mike Matson's neutral thesis is mainly based on Boston Scientific's (NYSE:BSX) cardiac implant, Watchman, for which a global trial called OPTION is currently underway involving about 1,600 patients who had undergone/ had been recommended ablation therapy for atrial fibrillation.

"New Medicare reimbursement and BSX's OPTION trial may drive increased concomitant use of WATCHMAN with ablation procedures, but without labeling/guideline changes, this may not drive a significant increase in WATCHMAN use," Matson wrote.

The analyst also pointed to risk in the company's ACURATE trial designed to evaluate its transfemoral ACURATE aortic valve platform, arguing that its outcome can move BSX shares up or down by 5-10% despite low investor interest.

Matson pulled his price target on the stock noting that Boston Scientific (BSX) shares are fairly valued and have exceeded Needham's previous price target.
